era_context: The year 1500 marks a pivotal moment in global history: the Portuguese have just completed the sea route to India; the Safavid movement is consolidating power in Persia; the Ottoman Empire dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; the Italian Renaissance is flourishing; and the Americas are on the cusp of sustained European contact. The Aztec and Inca empires remain at their territorial peaks, unaware of the impending arrival of European conquistadors.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal exploration not yet systematically documented)
Interior of the Americas (no European documentation; Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
East Asia and Southeast Asia (limited European sources; Chinese and Southeast Asian records exist but are underrepresented in Western scholarship)

events.title: Pedro Álvares Cabral's expedition reaches India; discovery of Brazil
events.region: Atlantic Ocean and Indian Ocean; Brazil and India
events.description: Pedro Álvares Cabral, commanding a Portuguese fleet of 13 ships, sailed from Lisbon in March 1500 with the goal of reaching India via the Cape of Good Hope. Due to winds and currents (or possibly deliberate navigation), Cabral's fleet was blown westward and reached the coast of South America (modern Brazil) in April 1500, making the first documented European contact with the region. After exploring the Brazilian coast and establishing initial contact with indigenous peoples, Cabral continued to India, arriving in Calicut in September 1500. His expedition confirmed the viability of the sea route to India and brought back spices and news of the newly discovered lands. The voyage was a triumph of Portuguese maritime technology and navigation.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and letters, particularly the Cartas de El-Rei Dom Manuel I
Pero Vaz de Caminha's letter describing the Brazilian coast and indigenous peoples
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese maritime expansion

events.certainty_note: Cabral's voyage is exceptionally well-documented in contemporary Portuguese sources, including the famous letter by Pero Vaz de Caminha. The dates (March departure, April arrival in Brazil, September arrival in India) are secure.
cross_references: 1501-002 (Portuguese establishment of trading posts in India)

events.title: Battle of Diu (First Battle of Diu); Portuguese naval victory in India
events.region: Arabian Sea, off the coast of Diu (modern Gujarat, India)
events.description: In September 1500, as Cabral's fleet approached India, a naval battle occurred off the coast of Diu between Portuguese ships and a combined fleet of Arab, Egyptian, and Indian vessels. The Portuguese, under Cabral's command, achieved a decisive victory, sinking or capturing several enemy ships. This battle demonstrated Portuguese naval superiority and established their military dominance in Indian Ocean waters. The victory facilitated Portuguese trading activities and the establishment of fortified posts along the Indian coast.
key_figures: Arab and Egyptian naval commanders (names not consistently recorded in Portuguese sources)

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and Cabral's own reports
Later Portuguese historiography, particularly by João de Barros

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in Portuguese sources. However, Arab and Indian sources provide limited contemporary documentation, so the battle is primarily known through Portuguese accounts, which may emphasize Portuguese achievements.
cross_references: 1509-001 (Battle of Diu, 1509, a later and more famous battle)

events.title: Death of John of Gaunt's line; succession crisis in England (Henry VII reigning)
events.description: In 1500, King Henry VII of England (r. 1485–1509) was consolidating his dynasty after the Wars of the Roses. The year marked a period of relative stability in England, with Henry VII strengthening his position through strategic marriages and alliances. His son Arthur, Prince of Wales, was alive and healthy, ensuring the succession. However, the broader context of 1500 in England was one of careful political management, as Henry VII worked to prevent rival claimants from challenging his rule. The year itself saw no major political upheaval, but it was part of the broader process of establishing the Tudor dynasty.

sources.name: Later English historiography, particularly by Edward Hall and Raphael Holinshed

events.certainty_note: The political situation in England in 1500 is well-documented in court records. However, 1500 itself was not a year of major events; it was a period of consolidation.
cross_references: 1502-001 (Death of Arthur, Prince of Wales, succession crisis)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; Ming Dynasty maritime expeditions
events.region: Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, East Africa
events.description: In 1500, the Ming Dynasty of China was in the midst of its great maritime expeditions under the command of the eunuch admiral Zheng He. Zheng He's treasure voyages (1405–1433) were among the largest and most sophisticated maritime expeditions of the pre-modern world, involving massive treasure ships and fleets that explored the Indian Ocean, visited East Africa, and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks. By 1500, these voyages were in their later stages, but they represented the height of Chinese maritime power and technological achievement. The voyages demonstrated Chinese naval superiority and the sophistication of Ming Dynasty navigation and shipbuilding.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424, patron of earlier voyages)
Hongwu Emperor's successors (patrons of later voyages)

sources.name: Ming Dynasty court records and the Mingshi (History of the Ming Dynasty)
The Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores) by Ma Huan, a participant in Zheng He's voyages
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Chinese sources. However, the exact dates and details of individual voyages are sometimes uncertain, and the voyages were largely abandoned after 1433 due to political changes in the Ming court. By 1500, the voyages had ended, though their legacy remained.

events.title: Montezuma II becomes Aztec Emperor
events.description: In 1502 (or possibly 1500, depending on the source), Montezuma II (also known as Moctezuma II) became the ninth emperor of the Aztec Empire, succeeding his uncle Ahuitzotl. Montezuma II would rule until his death in 1520, during which time he expanded Aztec territory and consolidated imperial power. However, his reign would be cut short by the arrival of Spanish conquistadors under Hernán Cortés in 1519. The year 1500 marks the beginning of a new era in Aztec history, though Montezuma II's succession is sometimes dated to 1502. Montezuma II is remembered as a complex figure: a capable military commander and administrator, but also a ruler whose indecision and superstitious beliefs may have contributed to the fall of the Aztec Empire.

sources.name: Aztec codices, particularly the Codex Mendoza and the Florentine Codex
Spanish chronicles by Bernal Díaz del Castillo and others
Nahuatl-language sources compiled after the Spanish conquest

events.certainty_note: The dating of Montezuma II's succession is uncertain. Most sources place it in 1502, but some Aztec sources suggest 1500. The Aztec calendar system and the conversion to the Gregorian calendar introduce uncertainty. The year 1500 is used here as an approximate date, but 1502 is more commonly cited in modern scholarship.
cross_references: 1519-001 (Arrival of Hernán Cortés and the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Cabral's 'discovery' of Brazil is sometimes overstated; indigenous peoples had inhabited the region for thousands of years, and the Portuguese arrival was not a discovery but a first contact from a European perspective. Additionally, the extent of Cabral's deliberate navigation versus accidental drift to Brazil remains debated among historians. Some scholars argue that the Portuguese were already aware of land to the west and deliberately sailed to investigate, while others maintain that the westward drift was unintentional. The Battle of Diu in 1500 is sometimes confused with the more famous Battle of Diu in 1509; the 1500 battle is less well-documented in non-Portuguese sources. Regarding Montezuma II's succession, the dating is genuinely uncertain, and the year 1500 may be incorrect; 1502 is more widely accepted in modern scholarship.
historiographic_note: The year 1500 is exceptionally well-documented for European history, particularly Portuguese maritime exploration, which is extensively recorded in royal archives and contemporary accounts. However, the documentation is heavily weighted toward European perspectives and interests. For the Americas, documentation is sparse and comes primarily from Spanish sources written after the conquest; indigenous Aztec and Inca records are fragmentary and were often compiled after European contact. For Asia, Chinese sources are abundant but are underrepresented in Western scholarship; the Ming Dynasty's maritime activities are well-documented in Chinese archives but less familiar to Western historians. African and Southeast Asian perspectives are largely absent from surviving written records accessible to modern scholarship. The year 1500 is often treated as a symbolic turning point in world history (the 'discovery' of the Americas, the beginning of European global dominance), but this periodization reflects a Eurocentric bias and obscures the continued vitality and sophistication of non-European civilizations at this time.

graph_edges.note: Cabral's successful voyage to India in 1500 led to the establishment of Portuguese trading posts in 1501.
The 1500 Battle of Diu established Portuguese naval dominance, leading to further Portuguese expansion and the more famous Battle of Diu in 1509.
Zheng He's voyages were largely complete by 1500, having ended around 1433.
Montezuma II's rule (beginning 1500 or 1502) ended with the arrival of Spanish conquistadors in 1519.
